description: Explains how Requests' default HTTP transport adapter connects Session requests to urllib3, how TLS verification and client certificates are translated into connection settings, and how proxy configuration is selected and applied.
document:
Requests uses transport adapters as the boundary between a Session and the actual network machinery. The user documentation describes adapters as per-service configuration objects: a session mounts an adapter on a URL prefix, and the longest matching prefix chooses which adapter handles a request. The built-in HTTPAdapter is the default for both HTTP and HTTPS sessions. It implements the small BaseAdapter contract by sending prepared requests, creating urllib3-backed connection pools, building Requests Response objects, and clearing pooled state when the adapter is closed.

In src/requests/adapters.py, the default adapter keeps one urllib3 PoolManager for direct connections and a cache of proxy managers for proxied connections. Its constructor records the pool sizing and blocking choices, normalizes the retry option into urllib3's retry object, and initializes the direct pool manager. The same saved pool settings are reused after pickling because the pool manager itself contains state that is not pickle-friendly. Closing the adapter clears both the direct pool manager and every cached proxy manager, which is the adapter-level cleanup path for pooled sockets.

TLS settings enter the adapter through the request-time verify and cert arguments. The helper _urllib3_request_context turns the prepared request URL, the verification choice, and an optional client certificate into two pieces of connection key material: host information and SSL-related pool arguments. Verification defaults to requiring certificates; passing false disables that requirement, while passing a path records either a CA bundle file or a CA directory depending on the filesystem. A client certificate can be a single path or a pair of certificate and key paths. The public certs module exposes certifi.where as the default certificate bundle source, and the advanced docs explain that certifi lets trusted roots be updated without changing Requests itself.

The older cert_verify path applies the same policy directly to a urllib3 connection object before sending. For HTTPS with verification enabled, it chooses an explicit CA location when supplied or falls back to Requests' default CA bundle, then fails early if that bundle path does not exist. If verification is disabled, it clears the CA fields and disables certificate requirements. When a client certificate is supplied, the adapter records the certificate file and optional key file and raises an operating-system error if either configured path is missing. The user docs also warn that disabling verification accepts any presented certificate and ignores hostname or expiry problems, and that encrypted private keys are not supported for client certificates.

Proxy handling is split between utilities and the adapter. In src/requests/utils.py, resolve_proxies starts with the request's explicit proxy mapping, respects a no-proxy setting when present, and may fill in a scheme proxy from environment variables when trusted environment configuration is enabled. The lower-level selection helper prefers a scheme-and-host entry, then a scheme entry, then all-proxy entries. The bypass helper supports host suffixes, host-with-port matches, plain IPv4 addresses, IPv4 CIDR ranges, and the platform proxy bypass hook; lowercase proxy environment variables take priority over uppercase variants.

Once the adapter has a proxy choice, it ensures the proxy URL has a scheme, rejects malformed proxy URLs without a host, and reuses or creates a matching proxy manager. SOCKS proxy URLs use SOCKSProxyManager when the optional dependency is installed; otherwise the placeholder raises an invalid-schema error explaining that SOCKS support is missing. HTTP proxy URLs use urllib3's proxy_from_url and may receive a proxy authorization header derived from credentials embedded in the proxy URL. The request_url method also adapts the outbound URL form: regular direct requests use the prepared path, plain HTTP requests through non-SOCKS proxies use the full URL with fragment and auth removed, and leading double slashes in the path are collapsed so urllib3 is not confused. The only adapter-specific test in tests/test_adapters.py covers that double-slash path cleanup.
